verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Moreover, you make a charge against yourself which has been brought by no one against you, and make excuses where no one has accused you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that you have read these and in my letter: “I want to know who has given you leave when translating a book, to remove some things, change others, and again add others.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And you go on to answer yourself, and to speak against me: “I say this to you Who I pray, has given you leave, in your Commentaries, to put down some things out of Origen, some from Apollinarius, some of your own, instead of all from Origen or from yourself or from some other?”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: All this while, while you are aiming at something different, you have been preferring a very strong charge against yourself; and you have forgotten the old proverb, that those who speak falsehood should have good memories.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that I in my Commentaries have set down some things out of Origen, some from Apollinarius, some of my own.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If then these things which I have set down under the names of others are the words of Apollinarius and of Origen; what is the meaning of the charge which you fasten upon me, that, when I say “Another says this,” “The following is some one’s conjecture,” that “other” or “some one” means myself?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Between Origen and Apollinarius there is a vast difference of interpretation, of style, and of doctrine.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: When I set down discrepant opinions on the same passage, am I to be supposed to accept both the contradictory views?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
